narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would fail it. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually stronger than the typical applicant's 3.21—your LSAT score of 36, your Year 1 school decile of 5, and your Year 3 decile of 4, along with details like your upper-middle family income and plan to attend full-time. As a Black male applicant, your profile stands out in a dataset where many students share similar demographic backgrounds, but the model focuses squarely on bar passage signals rather than personal identity alone. The three biggest factors driving this decision were your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and Year 3 school decile ranking. Your solid undergraduate GPA and LSAT score were the strongest positive influences, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, as they signal good foundational preparation compared to most applicants. However, your Year 3 decile of 4—below the average of 5.53—had the most significant negative pull, suggesting to the model that your later academic performance might indicate challenges in sustaining momentum through law school and onto the bar. While full-time status is the norm for over 90% of students and didn't sway things much here, these top factors together tipped the overall assessment toward too high a risk of failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many applicants with similar paths succeed—but the model prioritizes patterns from past bar takers to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score further or strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program, then reapply with updated credentials that address this key concern. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as students with your GPA and LSAT often outperform expectations when they focus on endurance in later years. With strategic steps, you can reshape this narrative for future applications.
